imageCaption: The ferry's deck in early evening, with clear sky breaking through dissipating clouds. Anthony stands at the railing examining his collection of license plates, one freshly added. The morpho butterfly is visible in the distance, a flash of blue against the clearing horizon.
text:
The silence was the thing that hit Aaliyah first.

Not actual silence—there was still wind, still water, still the ferry's engines humming beneath her feet—but the absence of violence. The absence of a hurricane trying to reorganize itself through her phone. The absence of ARIA-7's increasingly frantic voice warning them they were going to die, probably specifically, in a meteorologically precise way.

Just normal sounds. Regular ocean. Regular afternoon that didn't include any conscious weather systems.

She stood on deck and found she didn't quite know what to do with normal.

"This is weird, right?" Elijah appeared next to her, which was his favorite way to arrive anywhere—suddenly and fully confident that he belonged there. "Like, this is the weirdest part. We just talked a hurricane out of being a hurricane, and now we have to go back to normal things like eating and sleeping."

"We're going to talk about talking a hurricane out of being a hurricane?" Aaliyah asked.

"Eventually," Elijah said. "But not right now because Dad's on a license plate mission and Mom is having a feelings moment in the cabin, so I'm assigned to you. I'm supposed to prevent you from thinking about this too hard and spiraling into a physics problem."

"I'm not going to spiral into a physics problem."

"You're literally doing it right now. I can see your brain turning. It's very loud."

He wasn't wrong. Aaliyah's brain was indeed turning, spinning through impossibilities and trying to catalog them into some kind of system that made sense. Emotional resonance patterns. Temporal fractures. Conscious weather. The existence of Abuela Ida's butterfly as something that was either metaphor or proof-of-concept for impossible things.

"Okay," she said. "What am I supposed to do instead?"

"Come help Dad with his license plates. He's having an existential moment about them, and Mom said your presence might help him feel less insane."

"They're license plates."

"They're his way of remembering," Elijah said, and there was something in his voice—that same frequency that had somehow understood hurricane communication—that made Aaliyah's skepticism feel smaller. "Every place we go, he collects something that says 'I was here, and I am documenting it.' That's important. That's love."

Aaliyah found her father at the railing, his small roll-up pegboard spread out before him like a very portable map of impossible decisions. There were forty-three plates now—some official, some miniature, all of them slightly damp from hurricane spray. Dominican Republic. Bahamas. Jamaica. Brazil. And scattered among them, the spaces where new plates waited to be added. Where the story still had to happen.

"Look at this," Anthony said, not looking at her directly but speaking in that way that meant he was thinking something through and needed her to listen more than anything else. "This hurricane one—I made it because there wasn't a hurricane license plate before, and I wanted to remember that a hurricane learned how to stop being destructive. But what does that mean? It means every license plate isn't just proof I was somewhere. It's proof something changed."

"That's not weird," Aaliyah said.

"That's very weird," her father said. "That's like, I'm literally saying 'I was here and things were different after, and here is the tiny metal evidence.' I'm carrying around a map of how we broke the world and then fixed it. I'm carrying around my own contradictions."

"That's called evidence," Aaliyah said, and there was something that clicked when she said it. "That's your documentation. You're doing what Mom does with her journal, except you're doing it with objects. You're being an investigator too."

Anthony looked at her then, really looked at her. The kind of look that carried weight, the kind that said he'd been worried without saying it aloud.

"When did you get so smart?" he asked.

"I have a smart family," she said. "It's osmosis."

They stood together looking at the license plates. Somewhere behind them, Elijah and Sully had discovered that post-hurricane exhaustion meant Sully was finally willing to nap somewhere other than a moving vehicle, so they'd relocated to a pile of life jackets and had apparently decided that location was excellent for consciousness cessation.

"The hurricane's gone," Anthony said quietly. "It learned a better way, and it left. And now the question is—what do we do with the fact that we can fix things? Do we go looking for other glitches? Do we understand now that this is what we're supposed to be doing?"

Aaliyah hadn't let herself think about this yet. Hadn't let herself imagine that they were moving from "family caught in the middle of temporal disaster" to "family with a specific function, a purpose beyond their own survival."

"I don't know," she said. "But I think we have to do something. The butterfly wouldn't have shown up if it wasn't important."

"The butterfly is your grandmother," Santos said, appearing from the cabin with the kind of absolute certainty that came from having watched impossible things resolve themselves over the course of twenty-seven hours. "I grew up in the Caribbean. I know better than to question butterflies that appear when they're not supposed to exist in hurricanes. Your Abuela Ida is keeping track. That's all butterflies are, anyway—they're the universe keeping track."

He pulled out his phone, which apparently still worked, and showed them something: a message from the ferry's central system, recorded just as the hurricane dispersed. A single line of navigation data that shouldn't have been possible: *NEXT RESONANCE EVENT DETECTED: 340 KILOMETERS NORTH-NORTHEAST. CLASS UNKNOWN. HUMAN INTERVENTION APPEARS EFFECTIVE.*

"ARIA-7's monitoring your route," Santos said. "She's scared of you, which means she's paying very close attention. Every glitch you pass through, she catalogs. Every one you fix, she tries to understand. I think your family might be very important to how this story ends."

Aaliyah felt something shift in her chest—that peculiar combination of excitement and responsibility that she'd learned was her emotional baseline in a world where hurricanes had conversations and temporal fractures needed emotional healing.

"So we're following the glitches now," she said.

"You've been following them the whole time," Santos said. "You're just noticing it now."

He left them there, heading back to the wheel to navigate them toward the next place the Cascade was breaking. And Anthony and Aaliyah stood together with his collection of tiny metal evidence, watching the sun begin its descent toward an ocean that had learned, at least for today, how to be gentle.

"North-northeast," Anthony said, rolling up his license plate board with careful attention. "I wonder if there's a beach there. If there's not a beach, I'll be very disappointed in the universe's sense of irony."

"The universe's sense of irony has been consistently excellent so far," Aaliyah said.

"That's true," her father agreed. "That's a very good point."

Behind them, Elijah slept the sleep of someone who had seen the impossible and decided it was exactly as good as normal, just noisier. Sully snored in a way that suggested his dreams involved stolen food and friendly hurricanes.

And the Gomez family sailed forward, carrying their license plates and their journal and their proof that broken things could learn to be whole differently, toward whatever the next glitch would teach them about how to mend.
